title: Recognize and parse the real Claude subscription session-limit message
description: The quotad work (S-015) was built against a guessed usage-limit format and never fired on the real subscription message captured 2026-06-20: "You've hit your session limit · resets 1:10am (America/Toronto)". detect_usage_limit() missed it (no 'session limit' pattern) and _parse_usage_reset() returned None (the reset clause is neither a Unix epoch nor ISO-8601), so a real session limit was misclassified as an output-decline halt (S-017). Add a 'session limit' detection pattern and a 'resets <h>[:<mm>] <am|pm> (<IANA tz>)' reset-clause parser that resolves the next future UTC instant via zoneinfo, with tzdata declared so it works on Windows and a try/except fallback so a missing/unknown tz degrades to the configured cooldown rather than raising. Hand-implemented (not dogfooded) per the quota_resilience_plan tzdata risk note.
acceptance_criteria: detect_usage_limit recognizes the real session-limit message; the existing four patterns still match their samples; benign prose still returns False

_parse_usage_reset returns the correct future UTC instant for 'resets 1:10am (America/Toronto)' given a frozen now, rolls to the next day when that time already passed, and returns None (never raises) for an unknown tz or an unparseable clause
A real session-limit stage result routes through the quotad path (USAGE_LIMIT_HALT_REASON), not output-decline or dead-on-arrival
tzdata is a declared dependency so timezone resolution works on Windows; if absent, reset parsing degrades to None rather than raising
Unit tests cover detection, reset parse (frozen now, same-day roll, unknown-tz None), and the quotad routing; full suite uv run pytest -q stays green
